issues.claim: Never born, never dying; not having come to be, will not come to be again. Unborn, eternal, ever-existing, ancient — not slain when the body is slain.
issues.objection: The translation preserves all four predicates (aja, nitya, śāśvata, purāṇa) and the temporal-mode quadruple (jāyate / mriyate / bhūtvā / bhavitā). 'Ancient' for purāṇa is conventional and acceptable, though 'primeval' (Prabhupada) carries more of the prior-to-time sense. The verse's trishtubh meter and its near-verbatim relationship to Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.2.18 are preserved at the structural level (the source pack notes both).

issues.claim: ajaḥ nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṁ purāṇaḥ — unborn, eternal, ever-existing, ancient.
issues.objection: Sanskrit quoted verbatim with sandhi correctly resolved. Each predicate glossed accurately. The ṣaḍ-vikāra-denial frame Shankara reads onto these predicates is in the source pack but not surfaced in the engineering layer — a defensible scope choice for the analog register.

notes: The verse's role as the final naming of the substrate's full predicate-set is honored: all four predicates appear in the engineering layer with verbatim Sanskrit. The Upaniṣadic provenance (Kaṭha 1.2.18) is preserved in the source pack. Both Shankara's nondual reading (the four predicates deny ṣaḍ-vikāra) and Ramanuja's localised reading (the predicates rule out the body's attributes from the embodied jīva) are surfaced; the engineering layer stays inside cross-tradition consensus on the substrate-as-ancient claim. The 'customer-need predates everything' analog correctly anchors on purāṇa specifically. PASS.
